cards.body: Kapatid means 'sibling.' No separate word for brother or sister. Just kapatid.
Ko means 'my' or 'mine.' Put it after the noun.

example.note: Ko always comes after, never before. Not 'ko kapatid.'

cards.body: Mo means 'your.' Same rule: after the noun.

example.note: Ko = my. Mo = your. Both go after.

cards.explanation: Tatay ko = my dad. Ko (my) always goes after the noun.

cards.body: Now you can talk about anyone's family.

example.note: ko = my, mo = your. Always after.

cards.explanation: Mo = your. Kapatid mo = your sibling.

cards.prompt: Tell someone 'my older sister is Maria.'
cards.explanation: Ate ko si Maria. Ko (my) after ate, si before the name.
Kumusta kapatid mo = How is your sibling? Mo = your.

wrong_answer_tracking.behaviour: Wrong answers go to back of current session queue.

alam_mo_ba: Tagalog doesn't distinguish brother from sister. Kapatid covers all siblings. If you need to specify, you say 'kapatid na lalaki' (male sibling) or 'kapatid na babae' (female sibling), but most people just use ate/kuya/bunso.
Ko and mo are short forms. The long forms are 'akin' (mine) and 'iyo' (yours). You'll hear both.
'Si' before a person's name is like putting 'the' before a proper noun. It's just how Tagalog marks names in sentences.
